What   wi l l   we  cover   t oday?


Assess the future of business and organisational design through the liquid
enterprise concept


Unpack the key characteristics and features including value creation, time/
space, liquid structures, commoditization of data, and acceleration of linkages

Towards Liquid
Digital transformation and the future of business

What   i s  di gi t al i zat i on  busi ness  t r ansfor mat i on?


It refers to ev e r y   asp e ct  of doing business…


Digital business strategy
Digitally enabled business models
New digitally rev e nue   opportunities
Fully digital operations
End-to-end supply chains and
Digital customer relations and sales
Digital HR


Where digital was once an add-on, it is now mainstage, fully embedded

             commerce                                1980’s       networks
               began….

                                                                • Networks connecting business to business, business to consumers and consumers to consumers

                                                                                                                                                                                             6

Why  have  we  di gi t al l y  t r ansfor med?


Digital transformation has so far gone through three stages…



         Stage 1 (from late 1990s):                             Stage 2 (around 2000s):                                Stage 3 (around 2010s):


                •digital products (e.g., music,                        •digital distribution and web                           •mobile revolution, social media,
                 entertainment) and infrastructure                      strategy (e.g., e-commerce, online                     hyper digitization, and power of
                 (i.e., telecommunications,                             services)                                              analytics
                 software, information systems)

Organisations        increasingly empowered by the capability and convenience of accessing
information anytime and anywhere


Expectations have risen dramatically, and they have now become the primary force behind
digital transformation across different industries







                                                                                                                                                                          7

Wher e  ar e  we  headed  next ?



        Va lu e  C re a t io n  S p a c e s

            The Space-Time Dimension

             Plasticity of Organisational Spaces

            Va lo r is a t io n  o f d a t a

        Acceleration of linkages

Features and
Characteristics
Of the Liquid Enterprize

What   i s  ‘ val ue’ ?


Conventionally, value is perceived as a process             of consumption of an output (of tangible goods in
particular)


Val ue   cre ati on  ge ne ral l y   occurs within the boundaries of the organization


Val ue   cre ati on  op p ortuni ti e s have mushroomed with the advent of digital technology


There has been a wide expansion in businesses’ domain of value production











                                                                                                                                                 10

How i s the concept of val ue changi ng?


In the digital economy, value is created and deployed by multiple channels


Val ue   i s  cre ate d   by   an individual and collective experience


Val ue   cre ati on  i s  al so non-directly-transactional


Example: The value that a Google user creates is a consequence of their access to the site and
what they do when they are there. Value is not created only because the people use Google to
transact. Value is also created by the data people generate when they use Google










                                                                                                                                          11

What   ar e  val ue  cr eat i on  spaces?


This all suggests a new mode of production


Digital technology is a key component whose boundaries are yet to be determined


Borders of the enterprise have become fuzzy


Products and services are being developed in multiple spaces and multiple actors












                                                                                                                                 12

Wher e  wi l l   be  t he  new  oppor t uni t i es  for   val ue?


No longer limited to the traditional boundaries of the enterprise, value adding opportunities now
include:


           Competitors (i.e. shared platforms)
           Additional resources (such as those of suppliers or other industries)
           Customers
           Mobility
           Data
           Social spaces…
           …and the private time of employees

14

      How i s ti me-space being impacted?


      The ubiquity of digital technology has blurred the distinction between work and home


      Wo r k   t i me   i s  ex p and i ng


      Personal time is missing –     we all suffer nowadays from a lack of time


      A source of tension, but also of fundamental changes in the way things are “produced”


      This does not mean the disappearance of factories, or the modes of production traditionally
      associated with them


      But it does herald the ubiquity of productive time

Are organisations                       becomi ng ‘ li qui d’ ?


By ‘liquidity’ we mean the plastic (basically, malleable) character of the enterprise, its
boundaries, and its activities


The hybrid ‘bricks ‘n mortar and online model’ still prevails, but, we now see  ‘p u re l y   ’   d i g i t a l
businesses emerging


These are based on a business software and services model (e.g. Cambridge Analytica)


Able to more quickly develop adaptive offerings than more traditional businesses












                                                                                                                                             15

What   i s  t he  gener at i ve  nat ur e  of  t echnol ogy?


Enabled   by   d i gi t al   t e chnol ogy   i nf rast r uct ure   and   cl oud   p l at f or ms


Illustrates the generative      nature of digital technology –        capabilities inherent to a technology or
multiple technologies generate new products and services


Examples: drone, VR, mapping technologies, robotics, software –                   lead to the unitization of
commercial construction


See https://www.unitisedbuilding.com

Does thi s i nclude ‘ mobi li ty’ ?


Mobility  is essential in the design of the enterprise of the future and is one of the major
management challenges


Organisations will have to manage tensions between their mobile resources and fixed resources


Covid19  has accelerated the move toward a mobile (work-from-anywhere) workforce


Some organisations are now designing services with workforce mobility in mind













                                                                                                                                    17

What   i s  r ol e  i nst abi l i t y?


Role  = enterprise’s position in the productive system: customer, client, supplier, competitor


Conventionally, this position is deﬁned           within the industry  ’s value chain


With the digitalization of industries, the concepts of ‘value chain’ and, ‘industry  ’ are challenged


Example: A bank is traditionally a provider of credit/savings products and services,  but it can
also be… a supplier of customer; a consumer of data/ analytics service; an on-seller ie. provider
of ‘white label’ products and services


What role does Google        play in that value chain? What industry ?







                                                                                                                                                18

19
      Is this how liquid business will run?


      1) How do we manage         the new
      digitalized enterprise: Is the
      traditional vertical model with its
      long-term planning, activity-based                                                                                                C,C &P: Competitors,
      costing, cost and risk avoidance                                                                                                  Complementors and
                                                                                                                                        Platforms resources
      still applicable?


      2) Do we need a flatter
      management structure that
      embraces amongst other things,
      opportunism, collaboration with
      competitors, volatility and expects
      that some initiatives will fail?

How i s data changi ng?


Data is no longer just another resource


Data is now a source of value creation and also risk


Data is a monetizable asset


Data is, and will continue to be a major source of growth for organization


Data provides opportunities      for the production of new knowledge in new ways











                                                                                                                                    20

What   wi l l   make  dat a  so  chal l engi ng?


Not all data has the same value  For example, consider infrastructure data (e.g. system logs,…)
versus value creation data –             transactions, customer-side data, product data, service data, etc. …


Not all data is proprietary data For example, data may be a joint asset that is shared with others


Data must be mobile  The integration across multiple systems and platforms, availability of data
to end users across multiple devices and locations, the security of data, confidentiality of
customer and proprietary data and the valorisation                          (i.e. monetization) of data are critical to
digital business


Assessing the economic value of data is hard Ta      n      g      i      b      l      e             o      r             i      n      t      a      n      g      i      b      l      e             a      s      s      e      t      ?


Linking data to revenue generation is not always straight forward Need new programs for data
processing, in order to ensure consistency in terms of positioning, value and actions

What   i s  t he  dat a  ecosyst em?


Organisations    have to develop strategies and programs for data management, especially data
that is considered a strategic digital asset


Data needs to be understood as an ecosystem demanding…
           infrastructure
           applications
           devices
           mobility
           service providers
           decision-making processes
           management and governance processes and structures
           re g ul at or y   and   g ov e r nme nt   re q ui re me nt s




                                                                                                                                        22

                                                                                                                            More in
What   about   et hi cs,   r i sks,   and  secur i t y?                                                                    Week 10!


What about data ethics?
Some organizations collect data on everything regarding user  ’s behaviors
This data is monetized especially in B2B relationships
Behaviors   (such as search on Google and similar platforms) are source of monetizable knowledge
in advertising, marketing, publicity markets
This data can also be monetized in B2B relationship with suppliers


What about corporate responsibility or regulations?
Is there a need for the establishment of speciﬁc norms, regulations and standards for their
monetization?


What about security, privacy, and risks?
Privacy, Confidentiality, Security, being able to attest to good management practices



                                                                                                                                         23

How are linkages accelerating?


The acceleration of links is illustrated by the deployment of tools that shrink functional,
disciplinary and geographical distances


Market mechanisms (forces of demand and supply that determine price/ value of goods and
services) still prevail as key drivers of ideas and innovation, but…


Digital technology greatly contracts the space and time dimension of the links


Products and services get to market quicker


Organisations    able to modify offerings more quickly in response to customers







                                                                                                                                      24

Summary
Liquid Enterprizes

What   di d we  cover  today?
